title: Bonjour Is A Daytime Default
objective: Anchor bonjour as a context-sensitive greeting instead of a single English translation.
prompt: Read this: Bonjour is the safe default greeting during the day. It can mean hello or good morning, depending on context. Do not force a one-word English translation; attach it to the situation.
reveal: Key idea: bonjour is a daytime greeting. It is less about the English word hello and more about opening an interaction politely during the day.

title: Retrieve The Greeting
objective: Practice retrieval before seeing the answer.
prompt: It is 10:00 in the morning and you enter a bakery in France. What greeting would you use? Try to answer before revealing.
reveal: A good answer is: Bonjour. The context is daytime and a normal public interaction.

title: Bonjour Versus Bonsoir
objective: Build a contrast rather than memorizing two isolated translations.
prompt: Contrast these two situations: entering a shop at 11:00 versus entering a restaurant at 20:00. Which greeting fits each situation, and why?
reveal: 11:00 -> bonjour. 20:00 -> bonsoir. The important feature is time/context, not translating both as hello.

title: The Article Trap
objective: Expose a common misconception about grammatical gender.
prompt: Predict: if a French noun refers to an object with no natural gender, can you safely guess un or une from the object's meaning?
reveal: No. Grammatical gender is part of the noun. You usually learn the noun with its article: un cafe, une table. Meaning alone is not enough.

title: Use Articles As Part Of The Noun
objective: Turn the article rule into a practical habit for future vocabulary.
prompt: When learning a new French noun, what should you store: only the noun, or the noun together with un/une? Explain why.
reveal: Store the noun with its article. The article carries grammatical gender, so un cafe and une table are better memory units than cafe/table alone.
